chunk_summary: National Deworming Day focuses on safe mass deworming for children and adolescents aged 1–19 years, with exclusions for acute illness, severe malnutrition, allergies, or pregnancy. Preparedness for adverse events and proper administration protocols are critical for program success.
learning_objectives: Understand eligibility criteria for National Deworming Day, including exclusions for acute illness and pregnancy.
Learn proper albendazole dosing guidelines for children and adolescents aged 1–19 years.
Recognize adverse event preparedness strategies, including observation, referral chains, and choking prevention.
Explore microplanning essentials for safe and effective deworming campaigns.
key_facts: National Deworming Day targets children and adolescents aged 1–19 years with single-dose albendazole.
Dosage: 200 mg for children 1–2 years, 400 mg for children and adolescents 2–19 years.
Exclusions: acutely ill children, severe malnutrition with complications, allergy to albendazole, suspected acute surgical abdomen, and pregnancy in the first trimester.
Adverse events are typically mild, such as transient nausea or abdominal pain.
Choking prevention includes crushing tablets, proper positioning, and supervised administration for young children.
Microplanning includes administration after food, safe drinking water availability, observation post-dosing, and clear referral chains.
reference_quote: NDD targets children and adolescents 1–19 years with single-dose albendazole, delivered through schools and Anganwadi platforms with a planned 'mop-up day' for absentees. (Source: National Deworming Day operational guidelines)
document_key_topics: Eligibility criteria for National Deworming Day
